title: श्री तोटकाष्टकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री तोटकाष्टकम् ॥

विदिताखिलशास्त्रसुधाजलधे
महितोपनिषत्कथितार्थनिधे।
हृदये कलये विमलं चरणं
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥१॥

करुणावरुणालय पालय मां
भवसागरदुःखविदूनहृदम्।
रचयाखिलदर्शनतत्त्वविदं
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥२॥

भवता जनता सुहिता भविता
निजबोधविचारण चारुमते।
कलयेश्वरजीवविवेकविदं
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥३॥

भव एव भवानिति मे नितरां
समजायत चेतसि कौतुकिता।
मम वारय मोहमहाजलधिं
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥४॥

सुकृतेऽधिकृते बहुधा भवतो
भविता समदर्शनलालसता।
अतिदीनमिमं परिपालय मां
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥५॥

जगतीमविततां प्रभवप्रलयं
गततां परिवारगुणाश्रयतः।
भविता स्थिरशास्त्ररतिर्मम सा
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥६॥

जगदीशमनीशविहारमते
परिचिन्मयमेव विभान्ति जगत्।
नहि जातु मिथ्या भविता विभवे
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥७॥

महदादि जगत्प्रतिबिम्बमिदं
यदनन्तमजं परमात्मनि मे।
कृतसर्वहितं प्रवदत्प्रियतां
भव शंकरदेशिक मे शरणम्॥८॥

॥ इति श्रीतोटकाचार्यविरचितं तोटकाष्टकं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Totakashtakam ||

Viditaakhilashaastrasudhaajaladhe
Mahitopanishatkathitaarthanidhe.
Hridaye Kalaye Vimalam Charanam
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||1||

Karunaavaruna'alaya Paalaya Maam
Bhavasaagaraduhkhavidoonahridam.
Rachayaakhiladarshana Tattvaavidam
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||2||

Bhavataa Janataa Suhitaa Bhavitaa
Nijabodhaavichaarna Chaarumate.
Kalayeshwarajeevavivekavidam
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||3||

Bhava Eva Bhavaaniti Me Nitaraam
Samajaayata Chetasi Kautukitaa.
Mama Vaaraya Mohamahaajaladhim
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||4||

Sukrite'dhikrite Bahudhaa Bhavato
Bhavitaa Samadarshanalaalasataa.
Atideenamimam Paripaalaya Maam
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||5||

Jagatimavitaaam Prabhavaapralayam
Gatataaam Parivaaragunaashrayatah.
Bhavitaa Sthirashaashtraratirmama Saa
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||6||

Jagadeeshamaneeshavihaaramate
Parichinmayameva Vibhaanti Jagat.
Nahi Jaatu Mithyaa Bhavitaa Vibhave
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||7||

Mahadadee Jagatpratibimbam Idam
Yadanantamajam Paramaatmani Me.
Kritasarvahitam Pravadatpriyataam
Bhava Shankaradeshika Me Sharanam.||8||

|| Iti Shritotakaacharyaavirachitam Totakashtakam Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
(1) हे सम्पूर्ण शास्त्ररूपी अमृत के समुद्र! हे महान उपनिषदों में कथित अर्थों के निधि! मैं आपके विमल चरणों को हृदय में धारण करता हूँ। हे शंकर देशिक (आचार्य)! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(2) हे करुणा के समुद्र! भवसागर के दुःख से विदीर्ण हृदय वाले मेरी रक्षा कीजिए। मुझे सम्पूर्ण दर्शनों के तत्त्व का ज्ञाता बनाइए। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(3) आपके द्वारा जनता का कल्याण होगा। हे आत्मबोध के विचार से सुन्दर बुद्धि वाले! मुझे ईश्वर और जीव के विवेक का ज्ञाता बनाइए। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(4) 'आप ही शिव (भव) हैं' — यह मेरे चित्त में अत्यन्त कौतूहल उत्पन्न हुआ है। मेरे मोहरूपी महासागर को रोकिए। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(5) बहुत सुकृत (पुण्य) के संचय से आपकी कृपा से समदर्शन की लालसा उत्पन्न होगी। इस अतिदीन की रक्षा कीजिए। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(6) विस्तीर्ण जगत् को उत्पत्ति और प्रलय से युक्त, गुणों के आश्रय से परिव्याप्त जानकर, मेरी शास्त्र में स्थिर रति होगी। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(7) हे जगदीश्वर के विहार में रत बुद्धि वाले! यह जगत् चिन्मय ही प्रतीत होता है। विभव (ऐश्वर्य) में कभी मिथ्या नहीं होगा। हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।

(8) महत् आदि यह जगत् अनन्त अजन्मा परमात्मा में प्रतिबिम्ब मात्र है। सर्वहित करने वाले, प्रिय वचन कहने वाले, हे शंकर देशिक! मेरे शरण बनिए।
benefits: Exemplifies supreme guru-bhakti (devotion to the teacher)
Composed by Totakacharya in the unique Totaka metre
Seeks refuge in Shankaracharya as the embodiment of Shiva
Teaches the Advaita truth that the world is a reflection in Brahman
Develops humility, surrender, and eagerness for Self-knowledge
Invokes discrimination (viveka) between ishwara and jiva
Traditionally recited for removal of the ocean of delusion (moha) through guru's grace
description: Totakashtakam (श्री तोटकाष्टकम्) is an eight-verse devotional hymn dedicated to Lord Shiva. This revered text originates from Totakacharya (disciple of Adi Shankaracharya). Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to develop humility, surrender, and eagerness for Self-knowledge as well as teach the Advaita truth that the world is a reflection in Brahman. Additionally, it is known to exemplify supreme guru-bhakti (devotion to the teacher). This prayer is traditionally recited on Monday (Somvar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima and Maha Shivaratri to invoke divine grace. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 5 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Totakashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Totakashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Totakashtakam (श्री तोटकाष्टकम्) is an 8-verse Sanskrit guru-stotra composed by Totakacharya — one of the four principal disciples of Adi Shankaracharya — in praise of his master Sri Shankara, the great Advaita acharya. The hymn is the supreme exemplar of guru-bhakti (devotion to the spiritual teacher) in the Sanatana Dharma corpus, with each of the eight verses ending in the refrain Bhava Shankara-deshika me sharanam — Be my refuge, O Shankara, my divine teacher! The verses praise Shankara as the ocean of all shastras (Vidita-akhila-shastra-sudha-jaladhe), the treasury of Upanishadic meanings (Mahit-Upanishat-kathit-artha-nidhe), the ocean of compassion (Karuna-varuna-alaya), the giver of viveka between Ishvara and jiva (Kalaye-Ishvara-jiva-viveka-vidam), the dispeller of the great ocean of moha (Mama varaya moha-mahajaladhim), and the guru in whom the disciple recognises Shiva himself (Bhava eva bhavan iti — You are Shiva himself!). (Source: Adi Shankaracharya disciple tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Totakacharya — the composer of the Totakashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Totakacharya — originally named Anandagiri (or Giri) — was one of the four principal disciples of Adi Shankaracharya, along with Sureshvaracharya, Padmapadacharya and Hastamalakacharya. Tradition records him as a simple, devotional disciple of slow intellect who served Shankara with such pure-hearted bhakti that the master's grace flowed to him spontaneously. The famous legend recounts that one morning when Shankara was about to begin his Vedanta lecture, the other disciples wanted to start without waiting for the slow Anandagiri who was washing his master's clothes by the river. Shankara waited; when Anandagiri arrived running, the entire Vedanta tradition flowed into his heart by Shankara's grace alone, and he spontaneously broke into the eight verses of the Totakashtakam — composed in the rare totaka metre (after which he was renamed Totakacharya). The composition demonstrates that guru-bhakti alone, even without intellectual brilliance, opens the door to the supreme jnana. He became the first Mathadhipati of Jyotir Math at Joshimath in the Himalayas. (Source: Shankara-vijaya hagiographies; Madhaviya Shankara Vijayam.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Totaka metre and why is it special?
seoFaqs.answer: The Totaka chhanda is a rare Sanskrit prosodic metre composed of four sa-ganas (laghu-laghu-guru pattern) — that is, twelve syllables per pada in the pattern: ◡◡— ◡◡— ◡◡— ◡◡—, giving a galloping, almost dance-like rhythm. The Totakashtakam is composed entirely in this rare metre, and tradition holds that the metre itself is named after Totakacharya's spontaneous outpouring (totaka means he-who-prattles or he-who-chatters affectionately, the loving name Shankara gave to his simple disciple). The galloping totaka rhythm — bhava-shan-kara-deshika-me-sharanam — has eight syllables in each pada bound to the refrain, making the verses easy to memorise, melodic, and emotionally charged. The metre's brisk pace mirrors the disciple's eager rush to surrender at the feet of the guru. Many later Advaita stotras emulate this metre for guru-stotras precisely because of its association with the Totakashtakam. (Source: Sanskrit prosody (chhanda-shastra) tradition; Adi Shankaracharya disciple lineage.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Totakashtakam be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Totakashtakam is most powerfully recited on Guru Purnima (Ashadha Shukla Purnima — the supreme festival of the spiritual teacher), Shankara Jayanti (Vaishakha Shukla Panchami — Adi Shankaracharya's birthday), Maha Shivaratri (since Shankara is the Shiva-avatara identified with Bhava in the refrain), Monday (Somvar — the day of Shiva-Shankara), Thursday (Guruvar — the day of the guru-tattva), and on the day of one's own diksha-anniversary. It is the standard Sannyasa-Diksha stotra in the four Amnaya Mathas (Sringeri, Dwarka, Puri, Joshimath) — recited by every newly initiated Dashanami sannyasi when offering pranams to his guru. Householder disciples chant it daily before guru's photograph, especially before beginning Vedanta study sessions, and as part of the morning prayers in Advaita ashramas worldwide. The Totakashtakam concludes the daily nityopasana of many Smarta Brahmins of South India. (Sources: Adi Shankara monastic tradition; Sringeri Sharada Peetham.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Bhava eva Bhavan iti — You are Shiva Himself?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 4 of the Totakashtakam contains a profound declaration of guru-tattva: Bhava eva Bhavan iti me nitaram samajayata chetasi kautukita — That You are Bhava (Shiva) Himself was firmly established in my heart and my mind danced with wonder. This verse expresses the supreme realisation of guru-bhakti — that the human guru is not a mortal teacher but the visible form of Bhagavan Shiva himself, and that bowing to the guru is bowing to Shiva. The verse plays on the double meaning of Bhava — both Shiva (Bhava-Bhayanaka, the Lord) and the disciple's own becoming (bhava). The realisation Bhava eva Bhavan is the foundational insight of all guru-shishya parampara: as the Guru Gita declares, Guru-Brahma Guru-Vishnu Guru-Devo Maheshvarah, Guru-sakshat Param Brahma. Totakacharya's verse condenses this entire teaching into one ecstatic declaration. (Source: Totakashtakam verse 4; Guru Gita.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does daily recitation of the Totakashtakam grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition assigns the following blessings to sincere recitation of the Totakashtakam: (1) Guru-kripa — the personal grace of the spiritual teacher, even if one's own guru is no longer physically present, (2) the spontaneous awakening of viveka — discrimination between the eternal Atman and the transient world, (3) shelter from the moha-maha-jaladhi — the great ocean of delusion, as begged in verse 4, (4) Ishvara-jiva-viveka — clarity on the relationship between the supreme Lord and the individual soul (verse 3), (5) sthira-shastra-rati — firm love for and stability in the study of shastra (verse 6), (6) the example of supreme guru-bhakti — the devotional attitude that opens the heart to all spiritual teaching, (7) recognition of the world as a reflection in Brahman (verse 8 — mahadadi jagat-pratibimbam idam), (8) qualification for sannyasa or for the householder Vedanta path, and (9) the merit of having identified with the four blessed disciples of Adi Shankara himself. (Sources: Totakashtakam internal verses; Advaita guru-bhakti tradition.)
